items.prompt: How does Chapter 12 versioning differ in emphasis from Chapter 8 team-scale workflows?
choices: It focuses on lakehouse snapshots and object-store layouts at platform scale
It only repeats Git commit tutorials with no storage topics
It replaces all storage with handwritten logs
It removes any need for immutable version identifiers
items.explain: Section 12.3 states Chapter 8 holds Git/DVC tutorials; Chapter 12 focuses on distributed object stores and lakehouse table versions.

items.prompt: In Example 12.14, DVC hash files can live in Git while training shards live in an S3 or GCS remote.
items.explain: Example 12.14 describes that split between Git metadata and object-store bytes.

items.prompt: What does Pachyderm emphasize in Example 12.15?
choices: Recomputing only downstream stages that depend on a changed partition
Always rebuilding the entire lake on every tiny change
Deleting all version history after each run
items.explain: Example 12.15 describes selective recompute for changed partitions.

items.prompt: What does Delta Lake time travel avoid copying, according to Example 12.16?
items.rubric: Mention not copying every file / full table duplication; transaction log over Parquet on object storage.
items.sample_answer: It avoids copying every file by using a transaction log over Parquet on object storage.

items.prompt: Iceberg snapshots let operators point readers at a prior table state after a bad overwrite without a full warehouse restore.
items.explain: Example 12.17 describes snapshot rollback without restoring an entire warehouse backup.
